eras.eraDescription: NASA's founding and the intense Space Race era focused on human spaceflight. Key programs included Mercury, Gemini, and Apollo, culminating in the historic Moon landings and establishing U.S. leadership in space exploration.

items.title: NASA Founded
items.description: President Eisenhower signs the National Aeronautics and Space Act, establishing NASA on October 1, 1958.

image.alt: President Eisenhower presents commissions to NASA's first Administrator T. Keith Glennan and Deputy Administrator Hugh Dryden

items.title: Mercury 7 Astronauts
items.description: NASA introduces the first American astronauts, the Mercury Seven.

image.alt: Group portrait of the seven Mercury astronauts in their silver pressure suits

items.title: Alan Shepard First Flight
items.description: Alan Shepard becomes the first American in space aboard Freedom 7.

image.alt: Alan Shepard suited up before boarding the Freedom 7 Mercury capsule for his historic suborbital flight

items.title: John Glenn Orbits Earth
items.description: John Glenn becomes the first American to orbit Earth on Friendship 7.

image.alt: John Glenn inside the Friendship 7 Mercury capsule before his three-orbit flight around Earth

items.title: Project Gemini Begins
items.description: Gemini program starts, developing critical techniques for Apollo Moon landings.

image.alt: Gemini spacecraft and astronauts during an extravehicular activity in orbit above Earth

items.title: Apollo 8 Moon Orbit
items.description: Apollo 8 becomes the first crewed spacecraft to orbit the Moon and capture Earthrise.

image.alt: The iconic Earthrise photograph showing Earth rising above the lunar horizon, taken by Apollo 8 astronauts

items.title: Apollo 11 Moon Landing
items.description: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin become the first humans to walk on the Moon.

image.alt: Buzz Aldrin stands on the lunar surface during the Apollo 11 moonwalk, with the Eagle lander and equipment visible behind him

items.title: Apollo 12 Second Landing
items.description: Second successful crewed Moon landing, with precise touchdown near Surveyor 3.

image.alt: Apollo 12 astronaut on the lunar surface near the Surveyor 3 spacecraft during the second crewed Moon landing

eras.eraDescription: Post-Apollo transition to reusable spacecraft and planetary exploration. This era featured Skylab, the first Space Shuttle flights, international cooperation like Apollo-Soyuz, and major robotic missions including Voyager and Hubble's launch.

items.title: Mariner 9 Mars Orbiter
items.description: Mariner 9 becomes the first spacecraft to orbit another planet, mapping Mars.

image.alt: Mariner 9 spacecraft image of the Martian surface showing craters and geological features mapped during its orbital mission

items.title: Skylab Launch
items.description: America's first space station, Skylab, is launched and hosts three crews.

image.alt: Skylab space station in orbit above Earth, showing its solar arrays and the damaged micrometeoroid shield

items.title: Apollo-Soyuz Test Project
items.description: First joint US-Soviet space mission, symbolizing détente during the Cold War.

image.alt: American Apollo and Soviet Soyuz spacecraft docked together in orbit during the historic joint US-Soviet mission

items.title: Voyager 1 & 2 Launches
items.description: Twin Voyager probes launched to explore the outer planets and beyond.

image.alt: Artist's rendering of the Voyager spacecraft with its large dish antenna and instrument boom extended in deep space

items.title: First Space Shuttle Launch
items.description: Columbia (STS-1) marks the beginning of the reusable Space Shuttle era.

image.alt: Space Shuttle Columbia lifting off from Kennedy Space Center on STS-1, the first Space Shuttle mission

items.title: Sally Ride First US Woman
items.description: Sally Ride becomes the first American woman in space on STS-7.

image.alt: Sally Ride working at a control station aboard Space Shuttle Challenger during the STS-7 mission

items.title: Challenger Disaster
items.description: Space Shuttle Challenger tragedy leads to major safety improvements.

image.alt: Space Shuttle Challenger breaking apart shortly after launch on January 28, 1986

items.title: Hubble Space Telescope
items.description: Hubble is deployed, revolutionizing astronomy despite initial mirror issues.

image.alt: Hubble Space Telescope being released from Space Shuttle Discovery's robotic arm during its deployment in low Earth orbit

eras.eraDescription: Hubble servicing, International Space Station assembly, and renewed Mars exploration. This period emphasized international collaboration, repair of major observatories, and robotic missions that advanced our understanding of the solar system.

items.title: Hubble Repair Mission
items.description: STS-61 astronauts repair Hubble's mirror, enabling groundbreaking discoveries.

image.alt: Astronaut Story Musgrave anchored in the Space Shuttle payload bay during a spacewalk to service the Hubble Space Telescope

items.title: Mars Pathfinder & Sojourner
items.description: First rover on Mars explores the surface after successful airbag landing.

image.alt: The Sojourner rover on the Martian surface next to a rock, with the Mars Pathfinder lander visible in the background

items.title: ISS Construction Begins
items.description: First ISS modules (Zarya and Unity) are launched and joined in orbit.

image.alt: The Zarya and Unity modules of the International Space Station joined together in orbit, photographed from Space Shuttle Endeavour

items.title: Chandra X-ray Observatory
items.description: Chandra launches as NASA's third Great Observatory for high-energy astronomy.

image.alt: Chandra X-ray Observatory spacecraft in orbit, with its cylindrical body and solar panels extended

items.title: Spirit & Opportunity Rovers
items.description: Twin Mars Exploration Rovers land and begin extensive surface missions.

image.alt: One of the Mars Exploration Rovers on the Martian surface, with rocky red terrain stretching to the horizon

items.title: Cassini Arrives at Saturn
items.description: Cassini enters Saturn orbit for detailed study of the planet and its moons.

image.alt: Saturn and its rings photographed by the Cassini spacecraft, showing the planet's banded atmosphere and iconic ring system

eras.eraDescription: End of the Shuttle era and a shift toward deep space robotic exploration and commercial partnerships. Highlights include Pluto flyby, Curiosity landing, Voyager entering interstellar space, and Cassini's dramatic end.

items.title: New Horizons Launch
items.description: New Horizons launches on its journey to Pluto and the Kuiper Belt.

image.alt: New Horizons spacecraft launching atop an Atlas V rocket from Cape Canaveral on its journey to Pluto and beyond

items.title: Space Shuttle Program Ends
items.description: Final Shuttle mission (STS-135) concludes 30 years and 135 flights.

image.alt: Space Shuttle Atlantis touching down on the runway at Kennedy Space Center at the conclusion of STS-135, the final Shuttle mission

items.title: Curiosity Rover Landing
items.description: Curiosity lands in Gale Crater using the revolutionary sky crane system.

image.alt: Curiosity rover's first images from the surface of Mars inside Gale Crater, showing the rocky Martian terrain

items.title: Voyager 1 Enters Interstellar Space
items.description: Voyager 1 becomes the first human-made object to enter interstellar space.

image.alt: Artist's concept of Voyager 1 spacecraft crossing the heliopause and entering interstellar space beyond our solar system

items.title: New Horizons Pluto Flyby
items.description: Historic first close-up images and data from Pluto and its moons.

image.alt: Close-up image of Pluto captured by New Horizons showing the heart-shaped Tombaugh Regio nitrogen ice plain

items.title: Cassini Grand Finale
items.description: Cassini ends its mission with a dramatic plunge into Saturn's atmosphere.

image.alt: Artist's impression of the Cassini spacecraft making its final plunge into Saturn's atmosphere during the Grand Finale

items.title: InSight Mars Lander
items.description: InSight launches to study Mars' deep interior and seismic activity.

image.alt: InSight lander on the Martian surface with its solar panels deployed and seismometer placed on the ground nearby

eras.eraDescription: The Artemis era and next-generation observatories. Focus on returning humans to the Moon, powerful new telescopes like JWST, Mars sample return preparation, and expanding commercial crew operations to the ISS.

items.title: Perseverance Rover Launch
items.description: Perseverance launches with Ingenuity helicopter and sample caching system.

image.alt: United Launch Alliance Atlas V rocket carrying the Perseverance rover lifting off from Cape Canaveral Space Force Station

items.title: James Webb Space Telescope
items.description: JWST launches as the most powerful space telescope ever built.

image.alt: James Webb Space Telescope's first full-colour deep field image showing thousands of galaxies in unprecedented detail

items.title: Ingenuity Mars Helicopter
items.description: Ingenuity achieves the first powered flight on another planet.

image.alt: Ingenuity Mars helicopter hovering above the Martian surface during one of its early test flights, photographed by the Perseverance rover

items.title: Artemis I Test Flight
items.description: Uncrewed Artemis I successfully tests SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft.

image.alt: NASA's Space Launch System rocket carrying the Orion spacecraft lifting off from Kennedy Space Center on the Artemis I mission

items.title: OSIRIS-REx Sample Return
items.description: Asteroid Bennu samples returned to Earth by OSIRIS-REx.

image.alt: OSIRIS-REx sample return capsule shortly after landing in the Utah desert, containing asteroid Bennu material

items.title: Europa Clipper Launch
items.description: Europa Clipper launches to investigate Jupiter's icy moon and potential ocean.

image.alt: Artist's concept of the Europa Clipper spacecraft flying past Jupiter's moon Europa with its large solar arrays extended

items.title: Commercial Crew Operations
items.description: Ongoing crew rotations to ISS via commercial partners SpaceX and Boeing.

image.alt: SpaceX Crew Dragon spacecraft docked to the International Space Station with Earth visible below
